section.title: Simeon and the Transjordan Tribes
section.sectionRationale: The Chronicler turns from Judah to Simeon, the small tribe absorbed into Judah's territory, and then to Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, the tribes who settled east of the Jordan. Their stories close on the same somber note: unfaithfulness, and exile at the hands of Assyria, well before Judah's own exile to Babylon.
section.thesis: Simeon's quiet absorption into Judah and the Transjordan tribes' military strength, prayerful victories, and eventual unfaithfulness and exile together show the returned community both how smaller tribes can be preserved within a larger one and, more soberingly, how forgetting God even after real answered prayers leads straight to the very exile they themselves had just endured.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the descendants of Simeon; the cities and later expansion of Simeon; the descendants of Reuben and the lost birthright; the descendants of Gad in Bashan; the Transjordan tribes at war; and the half-tribe of Manasseh and the exile.

units.label: The descendants of Simeon
units.note: a tribe smaller than its brothers

verses.text: The descendants of Simeon: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul.
The sons of Shaul: Shallum, Mibsam, and Mishma.
The sons of Mishma: Hammuel, Zaccur, and Shimei.
Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters, but his brothers did not have many children, so their whole {{mishpachah|clan}} did not become as numerous as the sons of Judah.

units.label: The cities and later expansion of Simeon
units.note: settled among Judah, then seeking new pasture

verses.text: They lived in Beersheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual,
Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad,
Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag,
Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susim, Beth-biri, and Shaaraim. These were their cities until the reign of David.
And their villages were Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Tochen, and Ashan, five towns.
And all their surrounding villages as far as Baal. These were their settlements, and they kept a genealogical record:
Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah son of Amaziah,
Joel, Jehu son of Joshibiah (son of Seraiah, son of Asiel),
Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, Benaiah,
and Ziza son of Shiphi (son of Allon, son of Jedaiah, son of Shimri, son of Shemaiah).
These men listed by name were the leaders of their clans. Their families increased greatly,
and they journeyed to the entrance of Gedor, to the east side of the valley, in search of pasture for their flocks.
There they found rich, good pasture, and the land was spacious, peaceful, and quiet; for some Hamites had lived there formerly.

These who were noted by name came in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah. They attacked the Hamites in their dwellings as well as the Meunites who were there, devoting them to destruction even to this day. Then they settled in their place, because there was pasture for their flocks.
And five hundred of these Simeonites led by Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel, the sons of Ishi, went to Mount Seir
and struck down the remnant of the Amalekites who had escaped. And they have lived there to this day.

units.label: The descendants of Reuben and the lost birthright
units.note: the firstborn who forfeited his place

verses.text: These were the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel. Though he was the {{bekor|firstborn}}, his birthright was given to the sons of Joseph son of Israel, because Reuben defiled his father's bed. So he is not reckoned according to birthright.
And though Judah prevailed over his brothers and a ruler came from him, the birthright belonged to Joseph.
The sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.
The descendants of Joel: Shemaiah his son, Gog his son, Shimei his son,
Micah his son, Reaiah his son, Baal his son,
and Beerah his son, whom Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria carried into exile. Beerah was a leader of the Reubenites.
His relatives by their clans are recorded in their genealogy: Jeiel the chief, Zechariah,
and Bela son of Azaz, the son of Shema, the son of Joel. They settled in Aroer and as far as Nebo and Baal-meon.
They also settled in the east as far as the edge of the desert that extends to the Euphrates River, because their livestock had increased in the land of Gilead.
During the days of Saul they waged war against the Hagrites, who were defeated at their hands, and they occupied the dwellings of the Hagrites throughout the region east of Gilead.

units.label: The descendants of Gad in Bashan
units.note: a tribe recorded during the reigns of Jotham and Jeroboam

verses.text: The descendants of Gad lived next to the Reubenites in the land of Bashan, as far as Salecah:
Joel was the chief, Shapham the second, then Jaanai and Shaphat, who lived in Bashan.
Their kinsmen by families were Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jacan, Zia, and Eber, seven in all.
These were the sons of Abihail son of Huri, the son of Jaroah, the son of Gilead, the son of Michael, the son of Jeshishai, the son of Jahdo, the son of Buz.
Ahi son of Abdiel, the son of Guni, was head of their family.
They lived in Gilead, in Bashan and its towns, and throughout the pasturelands of Sharon.
All of them were recorded in the genealogies during the reigns of Jotham king of Judah and Jeroboam king of Israel.
The Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh had 44,760 warriors, valiant men who carried the shield and sword, drew the bow, and were trained for battle.
They waged war against the Hagrites, as well as Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab.

And because they cried out to God in battle, they were helped against their enemies, and the Hagrites and all their allies were delivered into their hands. Because they put their trust in God, He answered their prayers.
They seized the livestock of the Hagrites, 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, and 2,000 donkeys. They also took 100,000 captives,
and many others fell slain, because the battle belonged to God. And they occupied the land until the exile.

units.label: The half-tribe of Manasseh and the exile

verses.text: Now the people of the half-tribe of Manasseh were numerous. They settled in the land from Bashan to Baal-hermon (that is, Senir, also known as Mount Hermon).
These were the heads of their families: Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel. They were mighty men of valor, famous men, and heads of their families.
But they were {{maal|unfaithful}} to the God of their fathers, and they prostituted themselves with the gods of the peoples of the land, whom God had destroyed before them.

So the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria (that is, Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria) to take the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh into exile. And he brought them to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river of Gozan, where they remain to this day.

mishpachah.gloss: Clan or extended family. Simeon's clan did not grow as numerous as Judah's, a small, honest note reminding readers that not every tribe within Israel prospered equally, yet even the smaller mishpachah is recorded in full, because every family belongs to the whole people of God regardless of size.

bekor.gloss: Firstborn, with the double inheritance and leadership normally attached to that status. Reuben was Israel's actual firstborn yet forfeited the birthright through sin against his father, so that it passed to Joseph's sons, while the ruling scepter passed instead to Judah, another instance of God's blessing following His choice rather than birth order.

maal.gloss: Unfaithfulness, treachery, a breach of trust, most often used of covenant betrayal against the LORD through idolatry. The half-tribe of Manasseh's maal, prostituting themselves with the gods of the peoples God had already destroyed, directly precipitates the exile the Chronicler records in the very next verse, making this word the hinge between blessing and captivity.

basic.context: After three long chapters on Judah, the Chronicler turns briefly to Simeon, a tribe so small it eventually settled inside Judah's own territory and even sent out raiding parties to find fresh pasture as far as Mount Seir. Then the record shifts east of the Jordan to Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, tribes who chose to settle outside the promised land proper, in Gilead and Bashan. Their history is told with striking honesty: they were valiant warriors who won great victories because they cried out to God and trusted Him, yet in the end they turned to the gods of the peoples around them and were carried into exile by Assyria, generations before Judah's own exile to Babylon. A reader naturally asks why Simeon simply disappears into Judah, and why the very tribes praised for trusting God in battle end the chapter in captivity.
basic.hermeneutics: This is genealogical record combined with two short narrative summaries, the Simeonite expansion under Hezekiah (4:39-43) and the Transjordan tribes' war and eventual exile (5:18-26). Both summaries function as small case studies in covenant faithfulness and its opposite: prayerful dependence brings victory, and idolatrous unfaithfulness brings judgment, previewing on a smaller, earlier scale exactly what will later happen to Judah itself.
basic.originalLanguages: Simeon's mishpachah, clan, is honestly recorded as smaller than Judah's, a note of humility inside an otherwise proud genealogical record. Reuben's story turns on bekor, the birthright he lost through sin, so that the double portion passed to Joseph's sons while ruling authority passed to Judah instead. And the whole Transjordan section pivots on one devastating word, maal, unfaithfulness or treachery against God, the very charge that triggers the exile described in the chapter's last verse.
basic.typology: The Transjordan tribes' pattern, real victories won through prayerful trust in God followed by idolatrous unfaithfulness and exile, functions as a preview in miniature of Israel and Judah's whole national story, a pattern the New Testament resolves only in Christ, the true and faithful Israelite who never turns from His Father and so secures a kingdom that cannot be shaken.
basic.christ: Where Reuben forfeited the birthright through sin and the Transjordan tribes forfeited the land through unfaithfulness, Christ is the true firstborn, Colossians 1:15 and Hebrews 1:6, who never sins and never breaks faith with His Father, and so secures forever the inheritance that Reuben's sin and Manasseh's idolatry could not keep. Where these tribes cried out to God and were answered in battle (5:20), that same pattern of prayerful dependence finds its fullest answer in Christ, through whom every believing cry to God is heard.
basic.god: God is shown here as faithful to answer the prayers of those who trust Him in battle, even ordinary tribes on the edge of the promised land, yet equally serious about covenant unfaithfulness: the same God who delivered the Hagrites into Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh's hands for their trust later stirs up the king of Assyria against them for their idolatry. Both halves of this passage display the same God, gracious to the trusting and just toward the unfaithful.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 49:3-4 records Jacob's original judgment on Reuben for defiling his father's bed. Numbers 32 tells the story of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh choosing to settle east of the Jordan. 2 Kings 15:29 and 17:6 record the Assyrian exile of the northern tribes, the same disaster summarized here in 5:26.
basic.oneStory: Simeon quietly folds into Judah, and the Transjordan tribes rise to real victories through prayer only to fall into idolatry and exile a full generation before Judah's own captivity in Babylon. This early, smaller exile previews exactly the covenant pattern the returned community had itself just lived through, unfaithfulness answered by judgment, so that their own restoration after Babylon becomes even more remarkable evidence of a God who, though just, is also patiently faithful to bring His scattered people home again, ultimately through Christ.

context.literary: This section functions as a bridge between the long treatment of Judah (chapters 2 to 4) and the equally long treatment of Levi (chapter 6), pairing a small southern tribe, Simeon, with the three tribes that settled east of the Jordan. The two narrative asides, Simeon's expansion under Hezekiah and the Transjordan tribes' wars, interrupt the genealogical lists with real historical episodes that carry an explicit moral: verse 20 states plainly that God answered their prayers because they trusted Him, while verse 25 states just as plainly that unfaithfulness led to exile.
context.historical: Simeon's territory was absorbed early into Judah's own allotment, Joshua 19:1, so that by the time of the monarchy Simeonites lived, in effect, as a sub-group within Judah, which explains why the Chronicler records their later migrations as small, semi-independent expeditions rather than as tribal wars. The Transjordan tribes, Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh, had chosen to settle outside Canaan proper in Numbers 32, on the fertile pastureland of Gilead and Bashan, and were the first of all Israel's tribes to be carried into exile, by Tiglath-pileser (also called Pul) of Assyria around 732 BC, decades before the northern kingdom fell entirely and well over a century before Judah's own Babylonian exile.
context.canonical: This early Transjordan exile in 5:26 functions as a sobering precedent within Israel's canonical story: it shows that God's judgment on covenant unfaithfulness was not first exercised on Judah in Babylon but had already fallen, generations earlier, on these very tribes, establishing the pattern that 2 Kings 17 later applies to the whole northern kingdom and that Chronicles' original audience had themselves just experienced in Babylon.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record honestly both Simeon's smaller place within Israel and the Transjordan tribes' full history, from real, prayer-answered military victories to the unfaithfulness that brought Assyrian exile, so that the restored community could see in these tribes' story the same covenant pattern, blessing through trust, judgment through unfaithfulness, that had shaped their own recent history.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes specific tribal history; it does not prescribe holy war for believers today, nor does it teach that military victory always signals God's favor irrespective of later conduct. What it teaches enduringly is the abiding principle stated explicitly within the text itself: God delights to answer the prayers of those who trust Him, and covenant unfaithfulness carries real, historical consequences.

debates.question: Why does Reuben lose the birthright even though the sin (Genesis 35:22) happened a generation before this genealogy is written?

views.label: A settled, permanent forfeiture recorded as historical fact
views.case: Jacob's own deathbed words, Genesis 49:3-4, had already declared Reuben would not excel because he defiled his father's bed; the Chronicler is simply recording, centuries later, the outworking of that original, authoritative pronouncement, now visible in Joseph's sons receiving the double portion instead.

views.label: A case study in how one generation's sin can shape a whole line's inheritance
views.case: The passage functions less as a legal technicality and more as a sober illustration for the exiles themselves, that unfaithfulness has consequences reaching well beyond the individual who commits it, shaping the standing of an entire tribe for generations, a warning worth taking seriously by a community rebuilding its own identity after its own generational unfaithfulness had led to exile.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that Reuben's loss of the birthright is presented as just, rooted in Jacob's own words, and instructive: privilege and position are not automatic entitlements but can be forfeited, while God's larger purposes, here passing to Joseph and to Judah, continue regardless.

debates.question: How should readers hold together the Transjordan tribes' real, prayer-answered victories (5:20) with their subsequent unfaithfulness and exile (5:25-26)?

views.label: As two genuinely distinct moments, not a contradiction
views.case: The victories described in verses 18-22 belong to an earlier era (during the days of Saul, verse 10, and later), while the unfaithfulness of verses 25-26 describes a later spiritual decline; the text does not claim the same generation that prayed in faith is the one that fell into idolatry, so there is no contradiction, only a sobering record of how a tribe's spiritual condition can change across generations.

views.label: As a deliberate literary juxtaposition warning against presuming on past faithfulness
views.case: By placing the account of answered prayer and battlefield trust immediately before the account of unfaithfulness and exile, the Chronicler deliberately warns his readers that past experiences of God's favor are no guarantee of future faithfulness; a tribe can genuinely trust God in one generation and abandon Him in another, and readers must not assume their own community's past deliverances exempt them from ongoing vigilance.

debates.bearing: Both views draw the same practical lesson for the original, newly-restored audience: real answers to prayer in the past are not a permanent inoculation against future unfaithfulness, and each generation must renew, for itself, its trust in and loyalty to God.

study.originalLanguages: Simeon's mishpachah, clan, is candidly recorded as smaller than Judah's own, a humble note tucked inside a genealogy otherwise dominated by Judah's grandeur. Reuben's whole section turns on bekor, birthright, lost through sin and reassigned to Joseph's sons, while ruling authority passed to Judah, showing again that God's blessing tracks His choice rather than the accident of birth order. And the entire Transjordan narrative pivots on the word maal in verse 25, unfaithfulness or treachery, the precise covenant term for a breach of trust against God, which triggers, in the very next verse, the Assyrian exile the Chronicler records so soberly.

typology.type: Reuben, the firstborn who forfeited his birthright through sin
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true and sinless firstborn who never forfeits His inheritance and through whom believers receive an inheritance that cannot be lost, Colossians 1:15-18 and 1 Peter 1:4.

typology.type: The Transjordan tribes crying out to God in battle and being answered
typology.fulfillment: Every believer's confident access to God in prayer through Christ, our great high priest, who ensures our cries are heard, Hebrews 4:14-16.
The pattern of covenant breach and judgment that Christ, the faithful covenant keeper, absorbs and reverses on behalf of His unfaithful people, 2 Timothy 2:13.

study.christ: Where Reuben lost the birthright through sin and the Transjordan tribes lost their land through unfaithfulness, Christ stands as the true and better firstborn, Colossians 1:15, who never sins and whose faithfulness never wavers, so that the inheritance He secures for His people can never be forfeited as Reuben's was, 1 Peter 1:3-4. And where these tribes cried out to God in battle and were helped because they trusted Him, verse 20, that same pattern of dependent prayer answered by God reaches its fullest guarantee in Christ, our faithful high priest through whom we may approach the throne of grace with confidence, Hebrews 4:16, even when, unlike Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh, we have every reason to expect our own unfaithfulness, because His faithfulness, not ours, secures the outcome, 2 Timothy 2:13.
study.god: God is shown here as scrupulously just and remarkably responsive to faith in the same passage: He answers the Transjordan tribes' prayers in battle because they trusted Him, yet He also stirs up a foreign king to carry them into exile once they turn to other gods. Both actions flow from the same covenant character, a God who delights to help those who depend on Him and who will not indefinitely tolerate His people prostituting themselves with the gods of the very peoples He had destroyed on their behalf.

crossReferences.note: Jacob's deathbed pronouncement that Reuben would not excel because he defiled his father's bed, the background to the loss of the birthright in 5:1-2.
The account of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh choosing to settle east of the Jordan in Gilead and Bashan.
Tiglath-pileser's invasion and deportation of the northern territories, corresponding to the exile recorded in 5:26.
The fuller theological explanation of the northern kingdom's exile as covenant unfaithfulness, the same charge leveled against the Transjordan tribes here.

study.oneStory: Simeon quietly disappears into Judah's territory, and the tribes who settled beyond the Jordan rise to real, prayer-answered victories before falling into the very idolatry that leads to Assyria's exile, generations before Judah's own captivity in Babylon. This early, smaller-scale exile previews the whole covenant pattern the returned community had itself just lived through: unfaithfulness answered by judgment, yet never the final word, since the God who exiled these tribes is the same God who, in His mercy, was even then restoring their southern kinsmen and would, at last, secure an unshakable inheritance for His people in Jesus Christ, the true and faithful firstborn.

quiz.q: What happened to the tribe of Simeon's territory over time?

options.text: It was effectively absorbed into Judah's own allotment, so Simeonite clans lived within Judah's territory and later sent out expeditions to find fresh pasture.
options.note: The chapter records Simeonite cities inside Judah's region and later migrations to Gedor and Mount Seir under Hezekiah.

options.text: Simeon conquered and absorbed Judah's territory instead.
options.note: The reverse is true; Simeon settled within Judah's allotment, not the other way around.

options.text: Simeon remained the largest and most powerful of all the tribes.
options.note: The text explicitly notes Simeon's clan did not grow as numerous as Judah's.

options.text: Simeon's cities are listed, though the text does not explain their relationship to Judah.
options.note: The relationship is in fact implied by the cities named, which lie within Judah's allotted territory in Joshua 19.

quiz.q: Why did Reuben, though Israel's actual firstborn, lose the birthright?

options.text: Because he defiled his father's bed, so the birthright passed instead to the sons of Joseph.
options.note: 1 Chronicles 5:1 states this explicitly, echoing Jacob's words in Genesis 49:3-4.

options.text: Because he died before his father Jacob.
options.note: The text gives sin, not death, as the explicit reason for the loss of the birthright.

options.text: Because Reuben's descendants were simply fewer in number than Joseph's.
options.note: The stated reason is moral, sin against his father, not population size.

options.text: Because the ruling scepter, though not the birthright itself, passed to Judah.
options.note: True and stated in the same verses, but this option alone misses the specific reason given for the loss of the birthright to Joseph.

quiz.q: Why did the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh succeed in battle against the Hagrites?

options.text: Because they cried out to God and trusted Him, and He answered their prayers and delivered their enemies into their hands.
options.note: 1 Chronicles 5:20 states this cause and effect directly.

options.text: Because they had superior weapons and greater numbers than any other nation.
options.note: Their numbers and equipment are noted, verse 18, but the stated reason for victory is God's help in answer to their trust, not military superiority alone.

options.text: Because the Hagrites voluntarily surrendered without a fight.
options.note: The text describes a real battle with real casualties, not a voluntary surrender.

options.text: Because they made a political alliance with a neighboring kingdom.
options.note: No such alliance is mentioned; the explicit reason given is their trust in God and His answering their prayer.

quiz.q: What ultimately led to the exile of the Transjordan tribes?

options.text: Their unfaithfulness to the God of their fathers, prostituting themselves with the gods of the peoples God had already destroyed before them.
options.note: 1 Chronicles 5:25-26 states this as the direct cause of the Assyrian exile.

options.text: A random invasion unrelated to their spiritual condition.
options.note: The text explicitly connects the exile to their unfaithfulness, not to an unrelated event.

options.text: Their earlier decision to settle east of the Jordan rather than in Canaan proper.
options.note: Their location made them the first to face Assyrian incursion, but the stated cause of exile is their unfaithfulness, not the location itself.

options.text: Military weakness after years of decline.
options.note: The text does not attribute the exile to weakness; verse 18 in fact describes them as strong warriors, and the explicit cause given is spiritual unfaithfulness.

quiz.q: What does this section's pattern of trust, victory, unfaithfulness, and exile teach about God's character?

options.text: God is faithful to answer the prayers of those who trust Him, yet also just, bringing real consequences on covenant unfaithfulness.
options.note: Both halves of the passage display this same consistent covenant character.

options.text: God only cares about military victories and is indifferent to His people's faithfulness afterward.
options.note: The text explicitly ties the later exile to unfaithfulness, showing God's continuing concern for covenant loyalty.

options.text: God answered their prayers once but abandoned them permanently afterward regardless of their conduct.
options.note: The exile is tied specifically to their unfaithfulness, not to divine abandonment irrespective of their conduct.

options.text: God helped them in battle, and the text simply records their later exile without connecting the two.
options.note: The text draws a clear moral connection between their earlier trust and later unfaithfulness, which this option understates.

questions: Simeon was a small tribe absorbed into Judah's territory, yet still fully recorded and remembered by God. Where do you feel small or overshadowed by others, and how does this chapter reassure you that God still sees and records your place in His story?

Reuben lost his birthright through sin, a sober reminder that privilege can be forfeited. Is there a position of trust or privilege in your own life that you are at risk of losing through compromise, and what would it look like to guard it faithfully instead?

The Transjordan tribes won real victories because they cried out to God and trusted Him in battle. What battle are you facing right now where you need to cry out to God rather than relying only on your own strength?

The same tribes who once trusted God so visibly later turned to the gods of the peoples around them and were exiled. What habits or influences in your own life risk pulling you away from wholehearted trust in God over time?

This early exile of the Transjordan tribes previews the later exile the whole nation, including the Chronicler's own first readers, would experience. How does seeing God's justice and mercy across such a long history help you trust Him with disruptions or consequences in your own life right now?

translationNotes.note: The source text contained two em dashes around 'five towns' at the end of verse 32; these were rendered here as a comma and a sentence break, dividing the material across verses 32 and 33 as in the source verse numbering.
An em dash in the source before 'seven in all' was rendered here as a comma.
An em dash in the source before 'valiant men' was rendered here as a comma.
An em dash in the source before '50,000 camels' was rendered here as a comma.
Pul and Tiglath-pileser are identified in the text itself as the same Assyrian king, also called Tilgath-pilneser in some Hebrew manuscripts.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 4:24-43 and 5:1-26. Several em dashes in the source (4:32, 5:13, 5:18, 5:21) were converted to commas; none remain in this file.
